questions.question: In the F.I.R.S.T. acronym for a good unit test, what does the **R** stand for?
options: Repeatable — deterministic, with no reliance on `DateTime.Now`, `Random`, or real I/O
Realistic — exercises production data so results mirror real usage
Robust — keeps passing even when the production code changes
Reusable — the same test can be shared across multiple modules
questions.explanation: F.I.R.S.T. = Fast, Isolated/Independent, Repeatable, Self-validating, Timely. Repeatable means deterministic — no `DateTime.Now`, `Random`, GUIDs, or real I/O, so it produces the same result every run.

questions.question: Why should a unit test avoid `if`/`for`/`switch` logic in its body?
options: Branching in a test is a smell that the test is exercising more than one behavior
Test runners cannot compile control-flow statements inside `[Fact]` methods
Loops make the test run too slowly to qualify as Fast under F.I.R.S.T.
Conditional logic prevents the framework from generating a separate case per row
questions.explanation: Branching in a test is a smell that you're testing more than one thing — it breaks the one-logical-assertion / one-reason-to-fail discipline. Runners compile such logic fine; it's a design smell, not a syntax limit.

questions.question: xUnit creates a new instance of the test class for every test method, which is why it has no `[SetUp]`/`[TearDown]` attributes and uses the constructor and `Dispose()` instead.
questions.explanation: xUnit's signature isolation guarantee is a fresh test-class instance per test method, so the constructor serves as setup and `IDisposable.Dispose()` as teardown — no `[SetUp]`/`[TearDown]` needed.

questions.question: Which test double is best described as **a working, lightweight implementation** of a collaborator, such as an in-memory repository?
options: Fake
Stub
Dummy
Mock
questions.explanation: A fake is a working lightweight implementation (in-memory repo, SQLite-in-memory). A stub only returns canned answers, a dummy is an unused placeholder, and a mock carries pre-set expectations and fails if they aren't met.

questions.question: It is possible to reach 100% line coverage while the tests contain zero assertions.
questions.explanation: Coverage measures which lines/branches executed, not whether anything was verified. You can execute every line — hitting 100% — with no assertions at all, which is exactly why coverage is a poor quality target.

questions.question: An `OrderService.Place` method loads an order from a repository, then publishes an `OrderPlaced` event. You want to test that the event is published exactly once. Which choice best matches stub-vs-mock guidance?
options: Stub the repository (it's an input providing data) and mock the publisher (publishing is the behavior under test)
Mock both the repository and the publisher, with a `Verify` on every call to each
Stub both, then assert on the publisher's internal state after the call
Mock the repository to assert `GetById` was called, and stub the publisher
questions.explanation: The repository is an input that provides data, so a stub is right; publishing the event is the interaction that *is* the behavior under test, so a mock with `Verify(..., Times.Once)` is right. Verifying the query call (GetById) is the kind of interaction that is almost always wrong.

questions.question: A teammate writes `[InlineData(new Order(100m), 10m)]` on an xUnit `[Theory]` and it won't compile. What is the cause and the correct fix?
options: `[InlineData]` arguments must be compile-time constants; supply the `Order` via `[MemberData]` with a static source method instead
`[InlineData]` only accepts a single argument; split it into two separate `[InlineData]` attributes
`[InlineData]` cannot precede a `[Theory]`; move the attribute below the method signature
`[Theory]` cannot take reference types; mark the method `[Fact]` and loop over the cases internally
questions.explanation: Attribute arguments must be compile-time constants, so you cannot `new` an object inside `[InlineData]`. Use `[MemberData(nameof(Source))]` backed by a `static IEnumerable<object[]>` that constructs the instances at runtime.

questions.question: Why is writing a test method as `async void` dangerous?
options: The runner cannot await it, so unobserved exceptions and failed assertions are swallowed and the test falsely passes
`async void` forces the test onto a background thread, causing it to deadlock with `MockBehavior.Strict`
The compiler rewrites `async void` to run synchronously, defeating the purpose of an async test
`async void` cannot use `ReturnsAsync`, so the mocked dependency returns null
questions.explanation: An `async void` method can't be awaited by the runner, so unobserved exceptions and failed assertions are lost and the test passes falsely. Always use `async Task` and `await` the call under test.

questions.question: This xUnit test passes regardless of whether the order is actually saved. What is the primary bug?
options: `PlaceAsync` is not awaited, so `Verify` can run before the async work completes
`It.IsAny<Order>()` matches nothing, so the `Verify` is always vacuously true
`Times.Once` should be `Times.AtLeastOnce` for async methods
The mock needs `MockBehavior.Strict` or `SaveAsync` is never tracked
questions.explanation: `sut.PlaceAsync(42)` returns a `Task` that is never awaited, so the `Verify` can execute before `SaveAsync` is ever reached. Mark the test `async Task` and `await sut.PlaceAsync(42);` before verifying. `It.IsAny<Order>()` is a valid catch-all matcher, not the bug.

questions.question: Which of the following can Moq **not** override when building its runtime proxy? (Select all that apply.)
options: A non-`virtual` instance method on a concrete class
A `static` method
A member of a `sealed` class
An interface method
An `abstract` method
questions.explanation: Moq builds a runtime proxy and can only override interface, `abstract`, or `virtual` members. Non-virtual instance methods, `static` methods, and members of `sealed` classes can't be intercepted — which is why testable designs depend on interfaces/abstractions.

questions.question: What is the licensing situation a senior should know about FluentAssertions?
options: v8+ moved to a commercial license for business use, while v7 remains free under Apache 2.0
All versions are free under the MIT license with no commercial restrictions
v7 became commercial, so teams should upgrade to the free v8 release
It is dual-licensed GPL/commercial, so any closed-source use requires a paid license
questions.explanation: FluentAssertions v8+ moved to a commercial license for business use; v7 stays free under Apache 2.0. Many teams pin v7 or switch to Shouldly / AwesomeAssertions (a free fork).

questions.question: Which statements about xUnit `[Theory]` data sources are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: `[InlineData]` arguments must be compile-time constants
`[MemberData]` references a `static` property or method returning `IEnumerable<object[]>`
`[ClassData]` uses a class implementing `IEnumerable<object[]>`
`[Theory]` reports all its cases as a single combined pass/fail result
`[InlineData]` can construct `new` objects as long as the type is `sealed`
questions.explanation: `[InlineData]` requires compile-time constants; `[MemberData]` points at a static member returning `IEnumerable<object[]>`; `[ClassData]` uses a class implementing that interface. A `[Theory]` reports each row as a *separate* case, and `[InlineData]` can never `new` an object regardless of sealing.

questions.question: A repository test runs against the EF Core **InMemory** provider with no mocks. How should it be classified?
options: An integration concern — InMemory doesn't enforce relational behavior, so it isn't a true unit test of the database
A pure unit test, because the InMemory provider replaces the real database with an in-process double
An end-to-end test, because it exercises the full persistence stack
A unit test, since Microsoft recommends InMemory specifically for fast unit testing
questions.explanation: The EF Core InMemory provider does not make a repository test a unit test of the database; it doesn't enforce constraints, transactions, or raw SQL. Microsoft recommends testing against the real provider or SQLite — an integration concern.

questions.question: What is the deepest reason the GOOS principle says to avoid mocking third-party types you don't control (e.g. `HttpClient`, an SDK client)?
options: Mocking a foreign API bakes your possibly-wrong assumptions about its behavior into a test that stays green even when those assumptions are false
Third-party types are usually `sealed`, so Moq physically cannot create a proxy for them
External libraries change versions often, so the mock setup code needs constant maintenance
Mocking external types makes the test slower because the proxy must load the third-party assembly
questions.explanation: The core danger is that mocking a foreign type encodes your *assumptions* about its behavior; if those assumptions are wrong, the test passes anyway while production fails. The fix is to wrap it behind a thin interface you own and mock that. Sealing/version churn are secondary, not the principle's core.

questions.question: A reviewer flags a test that sets up six mocks and has eight `Verify` calls mirroring each internal method call, saying it will "break on any refactor." What is the best remedy?
options: Verify the observable outcome with state-based assertions and stubs, reserving `Verify` for the one or two interactions that genuinely are the behavior
Switch all six mocks to `MockBehavior.Strict` so the verifications become exact
Replace the mocks with concrete instances and keep all eight `Verify` calls intact
Add more `Verify` calls so every code path is fully pinned and refactors are caught early
questions.explanation: Eight verifies mirroring internal calls makes a brittle change-detector test that asserts *how* the code works. The remedy is to assert the observable behavior with state-based checks/stubs and reserve `Verify` for the interaction(s) that truly are the behavior (e.g. event published once). Six mocks also hints at an SRP smell.

questions.question: Two xUnit test classes mutate the same shared static cache. Each class passes when run alone, but the suite intermittently fails. Which fact about xUnit best explains this?
options: xUnit runs test collections in parallel by default, so the two classes execute concurrently and race on the shared state
xUnit reuses one fixture instance across classes, so the cache leaks between them
xUnit runs tests within a single class in parallel, so the static cache is corrupted mid-class
xUnit disposes shared statics between tests, so one class clears the other's cache
questions.explanation: xUnit runs test *collections* in parallel by default (tests within one class run serially). Two different classes touching the same mutable static run concurrently and race — the classic "passes alone, fails in the suite" bug. They must opt out of parallelism or share a collection. xUnit does *not* reuse a fixture across classes.

questions.question: In mutation testing (e.g. Stryker.NET), what does a **surviving mutant** indicate?
options: A line that is covered by tests but not actually verified — no test failed when the code was changed
A line that no test ever executes, so coverage is below 100%
A test that is flaky because it sometimes fails and sometimes passes
A piece of generated or trivial code that should be excluded from coverage targets
questions.explanation: Mutation testing mutates the code and checks whether tests fail. A surviving mutant means a change to a covered line did *not* break any test — proof the line is executed but not verified. That's a stronger quality signal than line coverage, which only shows execution.
